sanskrit:
नान्तोऽस्ति मम दिव्यानां विभूतीनां परन्तप |
एष तूद्देशतः प्रोक्तो विभूतेर्विस्तरो मया ॥४०॥
iast:
nānto'sti mama divyānāṃ vibhūtīnāṃ parantapa |
eṣa tūddeśataḥ prokto vibhūtervistaro mayā ||40||
explanation: There is no end to My divine manifestations, O scorcher of enemies. What I have declared is only a sampling of My boundless glory.
synthesis: Krishna steps back from the catalog and acknowledges its inherent incompleteness. Even after 30 verses of extraordinary examples spanning gods, sages, rivers, seasons, qualities, arts, weapons, and philosophical concepts — even after all that — He says: this is just a sample. The word 'uddeshatah' (by way of indication, as a pointer) is crucial: the catalog was never meant to be exhaustive but to train the mind to recognize the divine within excellence wherever excellence appears. The infinite cannot be cataloged; it can only be pointed toward. This verse teaches an essential epistemological humility: no list, no system, no tradition fully captures the divine. The teaching is always an indication, never a completion.

commentaries.text: Shankara notes that this verse formally acknowledges what the nature of Brahman requires: the infinite cannot be enumerated. All the examples given were pedagogical devices — shruti-vakyas (scriptural statements) designed to direct the mind toward the recognition of Brahman as the one ground of all excellence. Having served that function, they are released. The Advaita seeker is now ready to turn within and recognize the same ground in the witnessing awareness that observed all these manifestations.

Ramanuja sees this verse as an invitation to ongoing discovery: since the Lord's manifestations are infinite, the devotee's encounter with new expressions of divine glory is an endless, ever-refreshing journey. Every new creature, every new moment, every new quality of excellence discovered in the world is an encounter with an aspect of the Lord not previously known. The infinite nature of divine glory is itself a gift to the devotee — there is always more to love.

Madhva emphasizes that the infinity of divine manifestations points to the absolute and inexhaustible nature of Vishnu's sovereignty. No finite mind — human, angelic, or divine in any creaturely sense — can comprehend the full range of the Lord's creative and sustaining power. This is not an intellectual frustration but a call to ever-deepening reverence and the surrender of the presumption that one can 'figure out' God.

The bhakti tradition celebrates this verse as a declaration of the devotee's inexhaustible material for love. If the Lord's glories have no end, then the devotee's song of praise has no end. The saints — from Namdev to Tukaram to Meera — sang endlessly because they were encountering an inexhaustible source. Each new discovery of divine beauty in the world deepens rather than exhausts devotional ecstasy.

Abhinavagupta reads the infinity of manifestations as the infinite creative freedom of Shiva's self-expression. Consciousness (Shiva) plays (lila) by manifesting in endlessly various forms — not because it needs to but because its nature is creative effulgence. The practitioner of Pratyabhijna recognizes each new manifestation as a new face of the one Self, experiencing life as an ever-fresh encounter with Shiva's inexhaustible self-display.

For Vallabha, the infinity of Krishna's glories is the theological ground of a life of endless devotional discovery. No matter how long the devotee has loved Krishna, there is always a new aspect of His sweetness (madhurya), power (aishvarya), or grace to discover. This makes the bhakti path not a finite program but an eternal romance — each day bringing new encounters with the inexhaustible beloved.

Tilak reads the inexhaustibility of divine manifestations as a call to inexhaustible service. If the Lord's expressions of power and excellence are without end, then the karma yogi's opportunities for serving those expressions are also without end. No matter how much one has done, there is always more to contribute — not from anxiety but from the joyful recognition of an inexhaustible field of righteous action.

Vivekananda saw this verse as the ultimate corrective to intellectual arrogance. Every philosophical system, every religious tradition, every scientific framework is a 'sample' — an indication, not a completion. This should produce not despair but joyful intellectual humility: we are explorers in an infinite territory, and the joy of discovery never needs to end. The infinite nature of reality is an invitation to lifelong, passionate inquiry.

applications.personal_growth: Your potential has no end. Whatever you have achieved, whatever you have discovered about yourself — it is a sample, not a completion. The invitation is always to go deeper, to discover more of what you carry within, to let more of your seed-nature come to fruition.
applications.career_business: No body of work is ever truly finished — there is always more excellence to reach for. This is not a counsel of anxiety but of creative aliveness. The field of meaningful contribution in your domain is inexhaustible; approach it with joyful curiosity rather than pressure to 'complete' something.
applications.health: The body's capacity for healing, adaptation, and growth has not yet been fully mapped. Modern medicine is a sample, not a complete picture. Stay curious, stay humble, and keep exploring the full range of practices and approaches that support your flourishing.
applications.relationships: You will never fully know another person — their depth is inexhaustible. This is a gift: there is always more to discover in the people you love. Let relationships be ongoing explorations rather than completed maps.

personal_growth: What dimensions of myself remain unexplored that are calling for attention?
How do I relate to the fact that my growth has no final destination?
What would it mean to approach my life as an infinite exploration rather than a finite project?
How do I hold 'incompleteness' as a gift rather than a failure?
career_business: What aspects of my field remain unexplored that could be the next phase of my contribution?

How do I lead teams who understand that excellence is a journey without a final destination?

What does it mean to accept that my body's wisdom exceeds any system or protocol I follow?
How do I hold both the humility of not-knowing and the confidence to act on what I do know?

How do I avoid the trap of thinking I 'know' someone so well there's nothing new to learn?
Where have I stopped exploring in a relationship that is calling for renewed curiosity?

sanskrit:
यद्यद्विभूतिमत्सत्त्वं श्रीमदूर्जितमेव वा |
तत्तदेवावगच्छ त्वं मम तेजोंऽशसम्भवम् ॥४१॥
iast:
yadyadvibhūtimatsattvaṃ śrīmadūrjitameva vā |
tattadevāvagaccha tvaṃ mama tejo'ṃśasambhavam ||41||
explanation: Whatever being is glorious, prosperous, or powerful — know that each of these has arisen from a fraction of My splendor.
synthesis: This verse gives Arjuna — and every reader — a practical key to recognizing the divine in daily life: wherever you encounter extraordinary excellence, beauty, power, or prosperity, know that it is a fragment (amsha) of divine splendor. This is not a theological abstraction; it is a perceptual training. Look at the most brilliant scientist, the most moving work of art, the most courageous act of compassion, the most spectacular sunset — each is a tejas-amsha, a fragment of the same infinite radiance. The word 'amsha' (fraction, part) is crucial: these are not the fullness of the divine but sparks from it. And if the sparks are this astonishing, what must the source be? This verse prepares the ground for the culminating declaration of 10.42.

commentaries.text: Shankara explains that 'tejas-amsha' — a fraction of divine splendor — indicates that the world's glory is not independently real but is borrowed radiance, the reflection of Brahman's light in the mirror of name and form. The wise person, upon encountering any excellence, traces it back to its source in pure consciousness rather than stopping at the phenomenal expression. This recognition practice (viveka) steadily purifies the intellect and draws it toward non-dual recognition.

Ramanuja reads this as a guide to meditative devotion: any glorious being or thing in the world is a window into the Lord's even more glorious nature. The devotee uses the world's beauty as a ladder of ascent — not rejecting creation but seeing through it to the face of the Lord whose tejas (splendor) illuminates every exceptional being. This practice of seeing the Lord's glory in creation's excellence is itself a form of constant devotion.

Madhva emphasizes the word 'sambhavam' — arisen from. All creaturely excellence arises from, is dependent on, and ultimately returns to the Lord's independent splendor. This maintains the distinction between God and world while affirming their intimate connection. Every great being — human, angelic, or natural — is a demonstration of what dependent beings can manifest when the Lord grants His grace and power through them.

Bhakti poets turned this verse into a living practice: walking through the world and seeing Krishna's beauty in every beautiful thing. Mirabai saw Krishna in the lotus. Surdas sang of Krishna in every note of music. Tukaram found him in the faces of the poor. This perceptual transformation — the world becoming a continuous theophany — is the fruit of deep devotional practice and the gift of seeing through the heart rather than merely the eyes.

Abhinavagupta sees this verse as an invitation to pratibha — the direct, spontaneous illumination by which Shiva recognizes His own splendor in every phenomenon. When the practitioner encounters anything glorious, the recognition is not 'that belongs to God' but 'I am the source of that' — spoken from the identity with Shiva rather than from the contracted individual self. This is the practical application of Pratyabhijna: recognizing the divine as one's own deepest identity.

For Vallabha, every encounter with excellence in creation is an encounter with a fraction of Krishna's own effulgence. The devotee who has been trained in this seeing finds the world incessantly generous — it keeps showing new faces of the beloved. Flowers, music, acts of kindness, brilliant ideas — all are fragments of Krishna's splendor (tejas), pressing through the membrane of the ordinary to reveal the extraordinary that underlies all things.

Tilak translates this verse into a motivational and civilizational principle: the greatness achieved by exceptional human beings is a fraction of the divine's own inexhaustible excellence. This means human aspiration toward greatness is not hubris but participation in the divine nature. India's mission is to produce such greatness in every field — and the karma yogi who achieves excellence knows they are manifesting a fraction of God's own power, which comes with both dignity and responsibility.

Vivekananda called this verse the foundation of his teaching on the divinity of humankind. Every great person — every genius, every saint, every hero — is manifesting a fragment of the divine. And since all human beings share the same divine ground, everyone has the potential for such manifestation. The task is not to achieve divinity but to manifest the divinity that is already one's intrinsic nature. Encountering greatness in others should inspire expansion rather than envy.

applications.personal_growth: Train yourself to see divine fragments everywhere: in the person who moves you with unexpected courage, in the piece of music that breaks your heart open, in the natural world's inexhaustible beauty. This perceptual practice is a form of constant spiritual exercise that transforms how you experience ordinary life.
applications.career_business: Every truly excellent product, every breakthrough insight, every business built on genuine service is a fragment of divine splendor manifesting through human creativity. Approach your work as a channel for that radiance — not as an ego-project but as an expression of the excellence that wants to come through you.
applications.health: The body at its best — moving with grace, functioning with vitality, healing with remarkable intelligence — is a fragment of divine glory. Treat your physical life with reverence, as a sacred instrument through which a fraction of divine splendor can express itself in the world.
applications.relationships: The most luminous qualities in the people you love — their courage, creativity, kindness, humor — are fragments of divine splendor. Honoring these qualities in others is not flattery but recognition of what is truly present. Let this recognition deepen your reverence for the people in your life.

personal_growth: Where do I most consistently encounter divine fragments — what forms of excellence move me most deeply?
How does recognizing excellence around me as a fragment of the divine change my relationship to it?
What fragments of divine splendor are trying to manifest through my own life?
How do I practice the perceptual discipline of recognizing the sacred in the excellent?
career_business: What fragments of divine excellence am I most distinctively positioned to manifest through my work?
How does seeing my greatest professional achievements as tejas-amsha — fragments of divine splendor — change how I relate to success?
What glorious, prosperous, or powerful things in my field most clearly reveal the divine origin of excellence?
How do I build a culture where teams experience their best work as participation in something larger than themselves?
health: In what moments does my body most clearly reveal itself as a fragment of divine vitality and glory?
How do I cultivate reverence for the physical form as a temporary but genuine expression of divine splendor?
What would it mean to pursue health as a way of honoring the divine fragment entrusted to my care?
How do I see the extraordinary healing capacity of the body as a tejas-amsha?
relationships: Whose excellence — whose specific gifts and qualities — most powerfully reveal the divine to me?
How does recognizing the divine fragment in someone I struggle with change how I relate to them?
What fragments of splendor are the people I love carrying that I have stopped noticing?
How do I cultivate the discipline of recognizing and celebrating divine excellence in the people around me?

sanskrit:
अथवा बहुनैतेन किं ज्ञातेन तवार्जुन |
विष्टभ्याहमिदं कृत्स्नमेकांशेन स्थितो जगत् ॥४२॥
iast:
athavā bahunaitena kiṃ jñātena tavārjuna |
viṣṭabhyāhamidaṃ kṛtsnamekāṃśena sthito jagat ||42||
explanation: But what need is there for all this detailed knowledge, O Arjuna? I support this entire universe by pervading it with a single fragment of Myself.
synthesis: This is the culminating verse of the entire chapter — and one of the most staggering statements in the Bhagavad Gita. After thirty verses of carefully cataloged divine manifestations, after naming Himself in rivers and rivers of creation's finest moments, Krishna suddenly stops and says: why even bother with all of this? The whole universe — with its billions of galaxies, its unfathomable diversity of life, its entire span of time — is sustained by a single fragment (ekamsha) of the divine. The rest of Krishna — the unmanifest, the infinite, the beyond-beyond — remains untouched by creation entirely. This is not hyperbole; it is a cosmological and metaphysical claim of the highest order. The universe in all its vastness is not the full extent of the divine but an infinitesimal expression of it. What lies beyond the manifest universe — unmanifest, undivided, inexhaustible — dwarfs all creation as the ocean dwarfs a single raindrop. And yet that raindrop is real; it is just not the ocean. Every tradition responds to this verse differently, but all converge on awe.

commentaries.text: Shankara finds in this verse the final and definitive statement of Brahman's absolute transcendence: the manifest world, however vast and varied, occupies only a fraction (ekamsha) of what Brahman is. The remainder — if one can even use spatial language — is the infinite, undifferentiated, attributeless pure consciousness (nirguna Brahman) that is utterly beyond all manifest categories. The Advaita seeker takes this as the highest encouragement: the Self you seek to know is not exhausted by the universe — it contains the universe as a fraction, and its remainder is the deathless, boundless awareness that is your own deepest nature. This verse does not diminish the world but re-contextualizes it: seen from Brahman's perspective, the whole of creation is a single vibration within an infinite silence. The liberation the Advaita path promises is return to that silence — not the silence of absence but of infinite fullness that needs no further addition.

Ramanuja reads 'ekamshena' (with a single fragment) as pointing to the Lord's absolute sovereignty and inexhaustible power. The universe — which in Vishishtadvaita is the Lord's own body — is sustained by a single exercise of His will. The Lord's personal nature (vishishtata) — His qualities of knowledge, bliss, omnipotence, and grace — exceeds the universe immeasurably. For the devotee, this is not a cold philosophical statement but the most intimate of reassurances: the power that loves you and seeks your liberation is the same power that upholds galaxies, and to that power your welfare is not an afterthought but a central concern. The Lord who needs only a fragment to hold all of creation devotes the fullness of His personal attention to each devoted soul.

Madhva sees this verse as the supreme declaration of Vishnu's absolute independence (svatantrya). No being in creation — not Brahma, not Shiva, not any avatara or angel — participates in this sustaining act. Vishnu alone, by a single fraction of His inconceivable power, holds all that is in existence. Creatures are dependent (paratantra) in their very being; the Lord is independent (svatantra) in His. This inconceivable power does not make God remote — in Madhva's theology, it is precisely the God who is this overwhelmingly great who can also be intimately present to the individual soul as its inner ruler (antaryamin), closer than close, sustaining it from within with the same power that sustains the cosmos.

Bhakti saints have wept over this verse. Tukaram wrote: 'What more do I need to know? He holds the entire world with one hand and plays His flute with the other.' The image of Krishna — small, dark, playful, holding a flute in the forest of Vrindavan — as the same being who upholds the entire cosmos with a single fragment of Himself dissolves the devotee's heart entirely. This is the paradox that bhakti lives in: the infinite becomes intimate, the cosmic becomes personal, the power that sustains galaxies is the same that holds the devotee's heart in its palm. To know this is not to gain information but to be transformed — to fall into a love that is simultaneously intimate and infinite, personal and cosmic.

Abhinavagupta reaches his deepest register in commenting on this verse. The 'single fragment' by which the universe is upheld is the Spanda — the primordial divine vibration, the first throb of Shiva's self-awareness by which the unmanifest becomes manifest. The entirety of creation is this one vibration's elaboration. What remains beyond this fragment is the absolute silence of Paramashiva — pure, undifferentiated, beyond even the trace of the first vibration. Yet here is the mystery Abhinavagupta holds: even in the fragment, the whole is present. The recognition (pratyabhijna) of Shiva is not the discovery of something new but the recovery of what was never lost — the realization that one's own awareness is not a sliver of the fragment but the very whole of which the fragment is a part. The liberated consciousness does not occupy the fraction; it is the infinite from which the fraction arose.

Vallabha trembles with devotional wonder at this verse: the same Krishna who dances in Vrindavan, who steals butter, who plays with the Gopis, who answers Arjuna's questions with infinite patience — this same beloved holds the entire universe together with a single fraction of His effulgence. For Pushti Marga, this disproportion — the infinite contained in the intimate — is the very essence of Krishna's mystery. His grace (pushti) is not a fraction of His power but the full overflowing of a love that exceeds all cosmic measure. The devotee who has received even a drop of this grace holds, in that drop, more than all the universe contains. To be loved by Krishna is to be held by the hand that holds everything — with a gentleness that the cosmos, for all its vastness, does not experience.

Tilak draws a characteristically practical teaching from this cosmic statement: if the Lord upholds the entire universe with a single fraction of Himself, then the entire universe proceeds by a power that is neither exhausted nor in tension with itself. The karma yogi acts within a universe that is fundamentally held, sustained, and directed by an intelligence infinitely greater than any human understanding. This is the ground of fearless, unattached action: you are not carrying the universe — the Lord is. Your task is simply to play your role with maximum integrity, skill, and devotion, releasing all anxiety about cosmic outcomes. The equanimity of the karma yogi is grounded not in indifference but in the deep trust that the One who holds all things with a fraction of Himself is not asleep at the wheel.

Vivekananda read this verse and felt the full force of what he called 'the religion of strength.' If the divine sustains the entire manifest universe with a single fragment, then the source of that sustaining power is the deepest reality accessible to human consciousness — and every human being is made of that reality. This is why Vivekananda thundered 'Arise, awake!' — because the infinite sustaining power that upholds galaxies is sleeping within every human heart, waiting to be recognized. The spiritual life is not about acquiring divine power from outside but about removing the obstacles to the expression of the divine power that is already one's most interior nature. The verse also produces, for Vivekananda, the deepest humility: whatever any human being has achieved — the greatest science, the greatest art, the greatest service — is itself a fragment of the fragment. And that is not a diminishment: it is the greatest possible dignity, to be even a fragment of the inexhaustible.

applications.personal_growth: You do not have to carry the universe. The power that sustains galaxies with a single fragment also sustains you — from within, ceaselessly, without effort or fatigue. Let this truth be a foundation for deep rest: your growth, your healing, your becoming is held by something infinitely greater than your own effort. Align with it rather than exhausting yourself fighting against the current.
applications.career_business: Every great achievement of human civilization — every work of art, every scientific discovery, every act of organization and leadership that improved human life — is a fragment of the fragment. This does not minimize your contribution; it contextualizes it. You are a channel for an inexhaustible creative power. When you align your work with something larger than personal ambition, you tap into the same sustaining energy that holds the universe together.
applications.health: The body is sustained — from heartbeat to cellular repair to immune response — by the same intelligent power that holds the cosmos together with a single fraction of itself. This is not a metaphor but a recognition of the extraordinary intelligence embedded in biological life. The best medicine cooperates with this sustaining intelligence rather than overriding it. Trust the body's deep wisdom; support rather than override its innate movement toward wholeness.
applications.relationships: The love that binds persons together — in family, friendship, and romance — is itself a fragment of the sustaining love that holds the universe together. Even the smallest, most ordinary expression of genuine care participates in this vast sustaining power. Never underestimate what a kind word, a steady presence, or an act of forgiveness might be channeling. The fraction that holds the cosmos holds relationships too — you are not alone in the work of love.

personal_growth: If the same power that sustains the entire universe is also sustaining me right now, how does that change how I relate to my struggles?
What would it mean to stop trying to carry the weight of everything and instead align with the sustaining power that is already present?
How do I live in the recognition that my deepest nature is not a fragment but the source from which fragments arise?
What shifts when I stop seeking to become something and begin recognizing what I already am at the deepest level?
career_business: How do I align my professional work with the sustaining creative power that underlies all genuine human achievement?
If I am a channel for an inexhaustible source, what am I doing that blocks or contracts that flow?
What would it mean to build something that participates in the sustaining work of the universe — that genuinely upholds rather than merely exploits?
How does knowing that all my achievements are fragments of the divine fragment change how I relate to both success and failure?
health: How do I cooperate more fully with the sustaining intelligence already present in my body rather than fighting against it?
What practices help me align with the inexhaustible life-force that maintains biological existence?
How does the recognition of the body as a temporary, precious expression of divine sustaining power change how I care for it?
What would it mean to surrender my health anxiety to the same power that holds the cosmos together — not passively, but in active trust and cooperation?
relationships: How does knowing that the love between persons participates in the cosmic sustaining love change how I show up in relationships?
What would it mean to relate to the people I love as sustained by the same power that holds the universe — to see the sacred in their very existence?
How do I rest in the recognition that relationships, like the universe itself, are held by something infinitely greater than my individual effort?
What fragments of cosmic sustaining love am I most called to embody in my relationships right now?
